1: מִלְכֹּם (Milkom): the national deity of Ammon (cf. 1 Kings 11:5,7). The oracle's opening is a legal challenge: has Israel no heir to possess Gad's territory? The verb yarash (inherit, dispossess) is the covenant-land term used for Israel's original conquest. Milcom 'possessing' (yarash) Israelite territory is a theological affront — it is YHWH's land, and the Ammonite national god has no legitimate claim.
2: תְּרוּעַת מִלְחָמָה (teru'at milchama, war cry): the same term as the sacred shout (teru'ah) at Sinai and the signal for holy war. YHWH announces that he will deploy the war-shout against Rabbah (Rabbat Bene-Ammon, modern Amman) — the Ammonite capital. תֵּהְיֶה לְתֵל שְׁמָמָה (tehyeh letel shemamah, will become a heap of ruin): tel (mound) is the archaeological designation for ancient ruin mounds, still used today.
3: הֵילִילִי חֶשְׁבּוֹן (heilili Cheshbon, wail, O Heshbon): Heshbon was previously a Moabite/Reubenite city; the overlap of territories in these oracle poems reflects historical complexity. שַׂקִּים חִגְרוּ (saqim chigru, gird on sackcloth): the mourning garment. מַלְכָּם (Malkam): some manuscripts have melek (their king) and others Milkom (the god) — the ambiguity may be intentional: the god who was their 'king' goes into exile.
4: הַבַּת הַשּׁוֹבֵבָה (habbat hashsovevah, the backsliding/faithless daughter): the feminine personification of Ammon as a daughter who has turned away — shavav is the apostasy root. בָּטְחָה בְּאֹוצְרֹתֶיהָ (vatcha be'otzarotehah, trusted in her treasures): trust-vocabulary (batach) applied to material security rather than YHWH — the Ammonite error that mirrors Israel's recurring failure.
5: מְבִיא עָלַיִךְ פַּחַד (mevi alayikh pachad, bringing terror upon you): pachad is dread/terror — the covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:66-67. The full title Adonai YHWH Tzvaot (Lord YHWH of Hosts) carries maximum divine-warrior weight — the armies of heaven mobilized against Ammon.
6: וְאַחֲרֵי-כֵן אָשִׁיב אֶת-שְׁבוּת בְּנֵי עַמּוֹן (ve'acharei-khen ashiv et-shevut benei Ammon, but afterward I will restore the fortunes of the Ammonites): the same restoration formula used for Israel (Jeremiah 29:14; Jeremiah 30:3). YHWH's restoration purpose extends even to Ammon — the cup of wrath is followed by the possibility of return, countering the idea that the nations oracles are simply doom.
7: חָכְמָה בְּתֵימָן (chokhmah beteman, wisdom in Teman): Edom/Teman had an ancient reputation for wisdom in the ANE — Job's friend Eliphaz is from Teman (Job 2:11). The oracle's opening question — has Edomite wisdom perished? — is rhetorical: Edom's famous wisdom has failed to anticipate the judgment. Obadiah 8 uses the same motif.
8: עַמְקוּ לָשֶׁבֶת (amqu lashevet, go deep to dwell): the imperative to Dedan (an Edomite/Arabian tribe) to flee into the deep wilderness. אֵיד עֵשָׂו (eid Esav, the disaster of Esau): Edom consistently identified as Esau's descendants — the fraternal enmity of Jacob and Esau projected onto national history. The eid (disaster) echoes the cup-of-wrath theme (v12).
9: בֹּצְרִים (botzrim, grape-gatherers): the harvest analogy — even a grape-harvester leaves gleanings by covenant law (Leviticus 19:10). What will happen to Edom is worse: like nighttime thieves who take only what they want, but YHWH's stripping (chashafti, I have stripped bare) is total (v10). The legal harvest imagery contrasts the partial taking of human agents with YHWH's comprehensive judgment.
10: אֲנִי חָשַׂפְתִּי אֶת-עֵשָׂו (ani chasafti et-Esav, but I myself have stripped Esau bare): emphatic personal pronoun — YHWH himself, not Nebuchadnezzar, is the agent. Chashaf (strip/uncover) is used of removing a garment: Edom's protective covering is removed. גֻּלּוּ זַרְעוֹ (gullu zar'o, his offspring are devastated): the exile vocabulary, galah, applied to Edom.
11: עָזְבָה יְתֹמֶיךָ (azvah yetomekha, leave your orphans): YHWH will keep the orphans alive and the widows can trust him. The covenant care for the vulnerable (Deuteronomy 10:18; Jeremiah 27:19) extends even in the context of national destruction. YHWH's judgment on Edom does not revoke his care for the weak who survive it.
12: כּוֹס (kos, cup): the cup-of-wrath motif from Jeremiah 25. If those who did not deserve to drink (asher lo mishpat lahem lishtos et-hakos) were made to drink — i.e., Judah — then certainly Edom will not escape. Paul uses similar reasoning in Romans 11:21 (if God did not spare the natural branches, neither will he spare you).
13: נִשְׁבַּעְתִּי בִי (nishbati vi, I have sworn by myself): the divine self-oath formula used for the most irrevocable commitments — used for Abraham's blessing (Genesis 22:16) and the new covenant (Hebrews 6:13). Here it guarantees Bozrah's desolation. בָּצְרָה (Botzrah): the principal city of Edom, modern Buseirah in southern Jordan.
14: שְׁמוּעָה שָׁמַעְתִּי מֵאֵת יְהוָה (shmu'ah shamati me'et YHWH, I have heard a message from YHWH): the prophetic reception formula — the prophet hears before he speaks. An envoy (tzir) is sent among the nations to form a coalition against Edom. Obadiah 1 parallels this verse almost exactly.
15: קָטֹן נְתַתִּיךָ בַּגּוֹיִם (qaton netattikha baggoyim, I have made you small among the nations): the inversion of Edom's pride. בָּזוּי בָּאָדָם (vazui ba'adam, despised among human beings): the trajectory of pride → humiliation is the Proverbs principle of divine governance (Proverbs 16:18) operating at the national level.
16: זְדוֹן לִבֶּךָ (zedon libbkha, the pride of your heart): zadon (pride/insolence) is from the root of Pharaoh's hardening and Babylon's arrogance. שֹׁכְנִי בְּחַגְוֵי הַסֶּלַע (shokhni bechagvei hasela', you who dwell in the clefts of the rock): Petra/Sela, Edom's rock-carved mountain fortress. The security of the rock citadel is exposed as a delusion: YHWH's judgment is not blocked by topography.
17: שַׁמָּה תִּהְיֶה אֱדוֹם (shammah tihyeh Edom, Edom will become a horror): the horror-and-hissing formula (shammah + sherikah) is the standard covenant-curse expression for complete desolation, used repeatedly for Jerusalem and now applied to Edom. The rhetorical transfer: what Jerusalem suffered for covenant-breaking, Edom suffers for pride and violence against her brother.
18: כְּמַהְפֵּכַת סְדֹם וַעֲמֹרָה (kemahpekat Sedom va'Amorah, as in the overthrow of Sodom and Gomorrah): the Sodom/Gomorrah desolation as the paradigm case of total divine judgment. No one will dwell there: lo yeshev sham ish velo yagur bah ben adam. The same formula appears in 50:40 for Babylon — Edom and Babylon parallel recipients of Sodom-type judgment.
19: כַּאֲרִי יַעֲלֶה מִגְּאוֹן הַיַּרְדֵּן (ka'ari ya'aleh migge'on haYarden, like a lion coming up from the thickets of the Jordan): the same lion simile used in 4:7 and 5:6 for the enemy as YHWH's agent. מִי כָּמֹנִי (mi khamoni, who is like me?): YHWH's rhetorical self-assertion — the incomparability formula from Isaiah 40:25 applied to his judicial authority over Edom.
20: יַעֲצַת יְהוָה (ya'atzat YHWH, the counsel/plan of YHWH): Edom's fate is YHWH's deliberate plan, not accident. Etza (counsel/plan) is wisdom vocabulary — YHWH's sovereign governance operates through deliberate design. The sheep of Teman (tzon teman) will be dragged away — the shepherd-flock imagery of judgment.
21: מִקּוֹל נִפְלָתָם רָעֲשָׁה הָאָרֶץ (miqqol nifllatam ra'ashah ha'aretz, at the noise of their fall the earth trembles): the cosmic-scale earthquake response to the fall of a nation — similar imagery to Isaiah 13:13 (Babylon) and Revelation 18:19. The eschatological echo: cosmic disturbance marks the fall of the proud.
22: כַּנֶּשֶׁר יִדְאֶה וְיִפְרֹשׂ כְּנָפָיו (kannesher yid'eh veyifros kenafav, like an eagle he will soar and spread his wings): the eagle attack imagery — used for Nebuchadnezzar in Ezekiel 17:3 as the great eagle. Over Bozrah (al-Botzrah) — the eagle spreads its wings over Edom's capital as a bird of prey over its quarry.
23: חָמַת וְאַרְפָּד (Chamat ve'Arfad, Hamath and Arpad): Syrian cities — Hamath on the Orontes, Arpad in northwestern Syria. The shemu'ah ra'ah (bad news/evil report) causes dismay. הַיָּם (hayyam, the sea): their anxiety is like the troubled sea — uncontrollable agitation without resolution.
24: הֵרְפְּתָה דַמֶּשֶׂק (herpetah Dammesek, Damascus has grown feeble): the great Aramean capital enfeebled — the city that once threatened Israel (Isaiah 7:1-9) now flees. Chalchalah ve'chivlim (anguish and pangs) — the childbirth imagery for national catastrophe (cf. Jeremiah 4:31; Jeremiah 6:24).
25: אֵיךְ לֹא-עֻזְּבָה עִיר תְּהִלָּה (eikh lo-uzzevah ir tehillah, how is the renowned city not abandoned!): the elegiac eikh (how!) introduces a lament over Damascus's fall — the same opening word as Lamentations 1:1. Ir tehillah (city of praise/renown) — Damascus's reputation for civilization is used as a measure of the pathos of its destruction.
26: יִשְׁכְּבוּ בַחוּצֹתֶיהָ בַּחוּרֶיהָ (yishkevu bakhutzotyeha bachurehah, her young men will lie in her streets): the inverse of the city's vitality — bachurim (choice young men in their prime) slain in public spaces. The oracle for Damascus echoes the lament over Jerusalem (Lamentations 2:21).
27: וְהִצַּתִּי אֵשׁ בְּחוֹמַת דַּמָּשֶׂק (vehitzatti esh bechomatDammesek, I will set fire to the wall of Damascus): the fire-on-the-walls formula used throughout the nations oracles (Amos 1:4,7,10,12,14). בִּירֹנוֹת בֶּן-הֲדַד (biranot Ben-Hadad, the palaces of Ben-hadad): Ben-hadad was the dynastic name of Aramean kings (cf. 1 Kgs 20; 2 Kgs 6-8).
28: The oracle formula identifies the historical event: Nebuchadnezzar struck (hikkah) Kedar and the kingdoms of Hazor. Kedar = the Arab tribes of the northern Arabian Peninsula (Genesis 25:13; Isaiah 21:16-17) — descended from Ishmael; pastoral/semi-nomadic people, famous for their black tents (Song of Solomon 1:5) and archery (Isaiah 21:17).
29: אָהֳלֵיהֶם וְצֹאנָם (ohaleihem vetzonam, their tents and flocks): the specific material culture of the tent-dwelling Arab tribes — tents, flocks, curtains (yeriot), vessels (kelim), camels. The list of plunder enumerates the pastoral economy's wealth. מָגוֹר מִסָּבִיב (magor missaviv, terror on every side): Jeremiah's own signature phrase (Jeremiah 20:3-4,10) now applied to the nations.
30: נוּסוּ נֻדוּ מְאֹד (nusu nudu me'od, flee! go far away!): urgent triple imperatives. עָמַק עָצָה (amaq atzah, he has made a deep plan): Nebuchadnezzar's strategic intelligence is noted, but behind it is YHWH's counsel — echoing Isaiah 29:15's warning against hiding plans from YHWH.
31: גּוֹי שַׁלְאֲנָן (goi shala'nan, a nation at ease/living in security): the irony of security as vulnerability — a peaceful nation that has no defensive walls, gates, or bars (lo-delatot velo-beriach) is easy prey. Security built on prosperity rather than YHWH's protection is an illusion.
32: גְּמַלֵּיהֶם לָבַז (gemaleihem labaz, their camels for plunder): the camel economy stripped. קֹצְצֵי פֵאָה (qotztzei pe'ah, those who clip the corners of their hair): a distinctive ethnic identifier for certain Arabian tribes (cf. Jeremiah 9:26; Jeremiah 25:23). They will be zarah lekhol-ruach (scattered to every wind) — the diaspora language.
33: הָיְתָה חָצוֹר לִמְעוֹן תַּנִּים (hayetah Chatsor lime'on tannim, Hazor will become a haunt of jackals): when human habitation ends, wild animals take over. Tannim (jackals) are the emblematic inhabitants of ruins throughout the prophets (cf. Jeremiah 9:11; 10:22 for Jerusalem). שְׁמָמָה עוֹלָם (shemamah olam, desolation forever): not temporary exile but total abandonment.
34: The Elam oracle opens with a precise date: the beginning (bereshit) of the reign of Zedekiah. Elam was an ancient power east of Babylon (modern southwestern Iran). The oracle predicts Elam's defeat before the historical rise of Persian power (Elam was absorbed into the Persian Empire under Cyrus). The date formula grounds this among the earliest of Jeremiah's dated prophecies.
35: שֹׁבֵר אֶת-קֶשֶׁת עֵילָם (shover et-qeshet Elam, I will break Elam's bow): Elam was famous for its archers — their bow was their primary military asset and national symbol (Isaiah 22:6). Breaking the bow = destroying the nation's military power at its source. רֵאשִׁית גְּבוּרָתָם (reshit gevuratam, the foremost of their strength): the bow was Elam's qualitative edge in battle.
36: אַרְבַּע רוּחוֹת הַשָּׁמַיִם (arba ruchot hashamayim, the four winds of heaven): cosmic scope — YHWH will bring the four winds against Elam and scatter them in all directions. This imagery recurs in Ezekiel (Jeremiah 37:9) and Daniel (Jeremiah 7:2; Jeremiah 8:8) and Revelation's four winds (Revelation 7:1).
37: וְחַתֵּתִי אֶת-עֵילָם (vachatteti et-Elam, I will terrify Elam): chatt (terror/dismay) — the same root for the shattered self-confidence that judgment brings. The covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:25 ('YHWH will cause you to be defeated before your enemies') applied to the nations: YHWH's moral governance is universal.
38: וְשַׂמְתִּי כִסְאִי בְּעֵילָם (vesamti kis'i be'Elam, I will place my throne in Elam): YHWH's throne over Elam — the divine king's judicial seat established in judgment over foreign territory. The throne (kisse') is the symbol of legal authority. YHWH as universal sovereign has a throne that moves with his judicial activity — cf. Isaiah 66:1 and Ezek 1 (the mobile chariot-throne).
39: וְהָיָה בְּאַחֲרִית הַיָּמִים (vehayah be'acharit hayyamim, but in the latter days): the eschatological phrase. אָשִׁיב אֶת-שְׁבוּת עֵילָם (ashiv et-shevut Elam): the same restoration formula applied to Israel and Ammon (v6) is given to Elam. YHWH's purposes for the nations include their ultimate restoration — anticipating the eschatological vision of nations streaming to Zion (Isaiah 2:2-4; Revelation 21:24-26).
